g.text: Precedence Climbing Parser for: a OR b AND c > 5
Step 2: Unwind — no operator matches until parse_or_expr()
Step 3: OR consumed — recurse for right operand
Step 4: Inside parse_and_expr() — parse 'b AND c > 5'
Max Call Stack Depth at Each Token
Token 'b' | depth 5 | parse_primary (leaf, inside AND's left)
Deeper call = tighter binding = higher precedence

div.h2: Why AND Binds Tighter Than OR
div.p: parse_or_expr() calls parse_and_expr() for its operands. parse_and_expr() grabs everything it can (including c > 5) before returning to parse_or_expr(). OR never sees b, AND, c, >, or 5 individually — it receives the fully-built subtree (b AND (c > 5)) as a single right operand. The nesting of function calls IS the precedence.
Each precedence level calls the next-higher level before checking its own operator. parse_primary() consumes token 'a' and returns IDENTIFIER node.
OR's right operand is parsed by calling parse_and_expr(), which has HIGHER precedence. This is why AND binds tighter than OR — AND is parsed as a complete unit before OR sees it.

g.text: parse_and_expr() → no AND yet → returns Node(a)
parse_and_expr() — called for OR's right side
parse_primary() returns Node(b) — unwinds to AND
consumes AND, recurse for right operand
